explanation_md: `ptr` points to `b`. `ptr->width = 30` modifies `b.width` through the pointer. After the assignment, `b.width` is `30` and `b.height` is still `8`. `(*ptr).height` dereferences `ptr` to get `b`, then accesses `.height`, which is `8`.

options.a: `Point*` cannot point to a local variable.
options.b: `*ptr.x` is parsed as `*(ptr.x)`, which tries to apply `.x` to a pointer — a compile error.
options.c: Default member initializers prevent `Point` from being pointed to.
options.d: `std::cout` cannot print the result of a dereference.

explanation_md: `.` has higher precedence than `*`, so `*ptr.x` parses as `*(ptr.x)`. Since `ptr` is a pointer (not a struct), it has no `.x` member, causing a compile error. The correct expressions are `(*ptr).x` or, more idiomatically, `ptr->x`.

prompt_md: Given `Point p{ 1, 2 }; Point* ptr{ &p }; Point& ref{ p };`, which three expressions all access the same `x` member?
explanation_md: Values and references use `.`, pointers use `->`. So `p.x` (value), `ptr->x` (pointer), and `ref.x` (reference) all access the same member. Option `a` is wrong because `ptr.x` and `ref->x` swap the operators. Option `c` is wrong because `p->x` treats a value as a pointer. Option `d` has the precedence bug `*ptr.x`.

`doubleValue(&a)` passes a valid pointer; inside the function the null check passes and `a.value` becomes `10`. `doubleValue(nullptr)` passes null; the null check fails so nothing happens. `a.value` is `10` when printed.
